phase.title: PATH & DLL Hijacking
phase.summary: Hijack DLL search order or a writable directory in %PATH% to get code execution as a higher-privileged process.

nodes.title: Writable directory in system %PATH%
nodes.summary: Drop a malicious binary named after a command that admins/services invoke without absolute path.

snippets.title: Dump system PATH
snippets.code: for %i in (%PATH:;= %) do @echo %i & icacls "%i" 2>nul | findstr /i "everyone authenticated users"

nodes.description_md:
The Windows `%PATH%` environment variable lists directories searched when executing a program by bare name (without a full path). If any of these directories grants write access to a low-privileged user, the attacker can place a binary matching a command that administrators or privileged services invoke by name — without a full path — to hijack execution.

**Discovery**:
1. Print each PATH directory: `for %i in (%PATH:;= %) do @echo %i`
2. Test writability: `accesschk.exe -uwdqs Users C:\` or a PowerShell write-test loop
3. Identify commands admins call by name (tools in `C:\Windows\System32\` are safe, but custom entries like `C:\Tools\` may not be)

**Example attack**: If `C:\Tools\` is writable and admins run `cleanup.bat` from any directory, planting `cleanup.bat` or a malicious `cmd.exe` there hijacks the next invocation.

**Service hijacking variant**: Services that call helper binaries by bare name are excellent targets — they run as SYSTEM and don't require admin interaction.
nodes.detection_hints: New executables/DLLs created in PATH directories; process creation from unexpected locations; registry write to `HKLM\SYSTEM\...\Environment\Path` adding user-writable directories.

nodes.title: DLL Search-Order Hijack
nodes.summary: App resolves a DLL by name from its load path — drop a malicious DLL earlier in the search order.

snippets.title: Procmon filter
snippets.code: Operation = CreateFile AND Path ENDS WITH .dll AND Result = NAME NOT FOUND

nodes.description_md:
Windows DLL search order (with `SafeDllSearchMode` enabled, the default since XP SP2) checks locations in this order:
1. The application's directory
2. `C:\Windows\System32`
3. `C:\Windows\System`
4. `C:\Windows`
5. The current working directory
6. Directories in `%PATH%`

An attacker can hijack a DLL load if they can write to any directory that appears **before** the legitimate DLL's location in this order.

**Discovery** (Procmon-based):
- Filter: `Operation = CreateFile` AND `Path ends with .dll` AND `Result = NAME NOT FOUND`
- Observe which directories are probed before the DLL is found (or not found)
- Write a malicious DLL to the earliest writable location in the probe chain

**Key**: The DLL must export the functions the host application expects — either implement the legitimate API or use DLL proxying to forward calls to the real DLL.
nodes.detection_hints: DLL loads from unexpected directories (Sysmon EID 7); writes to directories before known DLL locations in the search order; new DLL files in application directories adjacent to signed binaries.

nodes.title: Phantom DLL Hijack
nodes.summary: A SYSTEM service references a DLL that doesn't exist anywhere — provide it.

snippets.title: Identify with Procmon
snippets.code: Find NAME NOT FOUND results for .dll requests from privileged processes.

nodes.description_md:
A phantom DLL is a DLL that a process attempts to load by name but that does not exist anywhere on the system. When a privileged process (SYSTEM service, scheduled task, or auto-elevating tool) has a phantom DLL dependency, any user who can write to any of the search-order directories can satisfy the load — and the DLL executes in the privileged process's context.

**Discovery** (Procmon):
1. Monitor the target service/process with Procmon
2. Filter: `Result is NAME NOT FOUND` AND `Path ends with .dll`
3. Note each missing DLL and which directories were probed
4. Check if any probed directory is writable

**Known phantom DLLs**: Some are historically well-known (e.g., `WLBSCTRL.dll`, `ntwdblib.dll` in the Windows system32 directory's predecessor path). These change with OS versions — always verify on the specific target.

**DLL template**: A minimal DLL just needs `DllMain` — no API exports required since the process won't call any functions (it'll crash or continue gracefully based on the `LoadLibrary` error handling).
nodes.detection_hints: DLL load from user-writable paths by SYSTEM-context processes; new DLL files appearing in locations adjacent to privileged binaries; Sysmon EID 7 with unexpected module paths.

nodes.title: Current-directory DLL load (legacy)
nodes.summary: Pre-SafeDllSearchMode apps load DLLs from CWD; useful on legacy XP/2003 boxes.

snippets.title: Concept
snippets.code: Drop a malicious DLL into the user-writable working directory the target app is launched from. Only effective when SafeDllSearchMode is disabled or the app uses LoadLibrary on a bare DLL name.

nodes.description_md:
Before `SafeDllSearchMode` was enabled by default (pre-XP SP2), Windows searched the **current working directory (CWD)** for DLLs before `C:\Windows\System32`. Applications that explicitly disable `SafeDllSearchMode` (via `SetDllDirectory("")` or the `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Control\Session Manager\SafeDllSearchMode = 0` registry setting) revert to the legacy behavior.

**Modern relevance**:
- Legacy applications compiled without SafeDllSearchMode support
- Applications that call `SetDllDirectory("")` to reset the search path
- Applications run from user-writable CWDs (e.g., `C:\Temp\`, `C:\Users\user\`)

**Attack**: If a privileged process starts in a user-writable directory, planting a DLL matching any of its imports there causes it to load the attacker's DLL before System32.

**Verification**: Check `SafeDllSearchMode` registry setting; use Procmon to confirm the CWD is probed before System32 for the specific target.
nodes.detection_hints: DLL loads from current working directories; processes starting from user-writable paths; registry setting `SafeDllSearchMode = 0`.

nodes.title: KnownDLLs & side-by-side abuse
nodes.summary: DLLs outside the KnownDLLs protection are resolvable from the app directory.
nodes.description_md: The `KnownDLLs` list pins system DLLs to System32, blocking hijack. Any import **not** in that list is resolved by the normal search order — so target non-KnownDLLs an app loads by name from its own directory.

references.title: MITRE T1574.001 — DLL Search Order Hijacking

nodes.title: COM server hijacking (HKCU)
nodes.summary: Per-user CLSID registration shadows HKLM, loading your DLL into elevated callers.
nodes.description_md: Windows checks `HKCU\Software\Classes\CLSID` before `HKLM`. Registering an `InprocServer32` for a CLSID an elevated/scheduled process instantiates loads your DLL in that context. A favourite persistence-to-privesc primitive.
nodes.detection_hints: HKCU CLSID\InprocServer32 writes pointing at user-writable DLL paths.

snippets.title: Register HKCU CLSID
snippets.code:
reg add "HKCU\Software\Classes\CLSID\{GUID}\InprocServer32" /ve /d "C:\Temp\evil.dll" /f
reg add "HKCU\Software\Classes\CLSID\{GUID}\InprocServer32" /v ThreadingModel /d Apartment /f

nodes.title: DLL sideloading via signed EXE
nodes.summary: Drop a malicious DLL beside a legitimate signed binary that loads it by name.
nodes.description_md: Many signed vendor binaries load a companion DLL from their own folder. If that folder (or an installer drop path) is writable, planting the named DLL gets your code executed under the trusted process — bypassing some allow-listing.

snippets.title: Find writable program dirs
snippets.code: Get-ChildItem 'C:\Program Files','C:\Program Files (x86)' -Recurse -Directory -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue | Where-Object { (Get-Acl $_.FullName).Access | Where-Object { $_.IdentityReference -match 'Users|Everyone' -and $_.FileSystemRights -match 'Write' } }

nodes.title: Find hijacks with Procmon
nodes.summary: Use Process Monitor to catch NAME NOT FOUND DLL/EXE loads from writable paths.
nodes.description_md: Filter Procmon for `Result is NAME NOT FOUND` + path ends with `.dll`, run by a privileged process. Each missing DLL in a writable directory is a candidate phantom-DLL hijack.

snippets.title: Procmon filter
snippets.code:
Filter: Result is NAME NOT FOUND
Filter: Path ends with .dll
Observe which privileged process probes a writable directory, then plant that DLL.

nodes.title: .NET assembly / config hijack
nodes.summary: Writable .NET app directories allow assembly redirection or DLL planting.
nodes.description_md: .NET resolves dependent assemblies from the app base directory and honors `<probing>`/binding redirects in the `.exe.config`. A writable app folder lets you plant a managed DLL or rewrite the config to load yours.

snippets.title: Targets
snippets.code:
- Plant <AppName>.dll dependency in the writable app base dir
- Edit <AppName>.exe.config <probing privatePath=...> to add your dir

nodes.title: Writable directory of a service binary
nodes.summary: If a SYSTEM service's own folder is writable, plant a DLL it imports.
nodes.description_md: Service executables load DLLs from their install directory first. A writable service folder is a reliable hijack: drop a DLL the service imports (or a phantom one) and restart the service.

nodes.title: DLL proxying (export forwarding)
nodes.summary: Forward legitimate exports to the real DLL so the host app keeps working.
nodes.description_md: To hijack without breaking the target, build a proxy DLL that forwards every export to the original (`#pragma comment(linker, "/export:Func=real.Func")`) while your `DllMain` runs your code. Keeps the app stable and evades crashes.

snippets.title: Export forward pragma
snippets.code:
#pragma comment(linker, "/export:DllFunc=real_orig.DllFunc,@1")
BOOL APIENTRY DllMain(HMODULE h, DWORD reason, LPVOID r){ if(reason==DLL_PROCESS_ATTACH){ /* run payload */ } return TRUE; }

nodes.title: Writable PATH entry hijack
nodes.summary: An early writable PATH directory wins name resolution for EXE/DLL lookups.
nodes.description_md: When a privileged process launches a helper by bare name (no full path), the first PATH directory containing a match wins. A user-writable directory early in the system PATH lets you plant a same-named binary.

snippets.title: Writable PATH dirs
snippets.code: $env:Path -split ';' | ForEach-Object { if (Test-Path $_) { try { $t=Join-Path $_ 'w.tmp'; New-Item $t -ItemType File -EA Stop | Out-Null; Remove-Item $t; "WRITABLE: $_" } catch {} } }
